ab17680e6c8c:
Machine-learned strategy: feature extraction (honestly observable, fixed length)
plus linear inference by argmax, so it introduces no randomness and stays
deterministic.
28d147bddd91:
Kiting was never used in practice, so it is now chosen explicitly: a group that
is mostly ranged and mobile, facing a locally observed majority of melee, is the
classic kiting matchup and switches to that engagement rule; when the conditions
go away it switches back. Two thresholds provide hysteresis.
bb82589a2de6:
Garrisoning troops. Watching games showed the AI never used its own buildings:
a damaged group under threat now shelters in a nearby fortification, which both
protects it and adds arrows, and comes out when it has healed or the threat has
gone. The 40/75% hysteresis matches the villagers' repair logic.
This is a group-level order, deliberately: a per-unit version would give the AI a
gesture the player does not have.
26b7710d0ee5:
Entry condition: the group's *average* health is low, it is under threat, and
there is a fortification with room near its centroid.
8c6db8dd5e11:
Shared: the friendly fortification nearest a point. It doubles as the retreat
point for cut-off siege and the regrouping point for the main force, so wounded
units falling back can shelter inside on the way. With no fortification, the home
rally point.
1d60998ac226:
Breaking into a defended base. Above hard, the default assault mode nibbles at
the perimeter rather than driving at the command centre.
95f118d5b525:
Thinnest-point scan: among known enemy defences within reach of the advance
anchor and closer to the enemy centre than the anchor is -- so progress is always
forward -- take the one with the lowest local defence score. "Thinnest" means
taking the least crossfire while attacking it.
Everything is read through the side's own intelligence, so an unscouted tower
does not count. Picking the weak point is intelligence paying off, not peeking.
90e577f20982:
Cut-off siege retreats. A catapult needs 2.5s to set up, loses it on any
movement, is the slowest thing on the field, the most fragile, and costs three
times an infantryman -- unescorted, it is iron given away. When the friendly
combat strength around it stays below the escort threshold for several seconds
(brief passes do not count), the whole siege group pulls back.
6e95cdea997c:
Three-squad military entry point. With the ablation switch off it falls back to
the previous multi-group implementation, byte for byte, as a control.
86016fde8a45: The previous multi-group implementation, kept as the ablation baseline.
c60ff5035f2d:
Intelligence, under the same vision rules as the player: enemy and friendly
strength, plus a slowly decaying memory of enemy composition, so an army that
walks out of sight is not instantly forgotten.
db9d591f3d94:
Home threat scan, which triggers defence and the response to a raided mine:
visible enemies near our buildings or villagers give a threat centroid.
ec6ee659e2c6:
Two signals that the moment is right, both from actual sightings.
One: the opponent has just fought and taken losses -- their observed strength is
far below its recent peak while ours is still near ours. In a duel that usually
means their army broke on our fortifications while we stayed intact, so the
threshold to attack drops inside that window.
Two: their main force is far from home, which is an opening.
6c2a310608e4:
The attack/defend state machine (develop -> pressure -> all-in, plus defending
and retreat) with a timeout clause for an opponent who turtles indefinitely.
Every threshold is read from the difficulty knobs and the opening template.
56967f3b5f5d:
Wave decisions look at the main force rather than the whole army. Counting every
unit meant that after the main force died, the guards and patrols still totalled
enough to re-enter the pressure state immediately -- so every newly produced unit
walked to the old rally point on its own and was killed. Now new units gather at
home until they are a wave.
02f067e4c974:
The easier difficulties do not compare strengths before attacking; they simply
wait for a wave. Running into a large army or a dense line of fortifications
raises the wave threshold rather than cancelling the attack -- same knob, no new
mechanism.
f0a8c4fae0fc:
Threshold for entering the defensive state, deliberately raised. At the old
value, two spearmen within nine tiles of any building at all flipped the entire
brain to defending -- and with farms and outposts scattered across the map, a
passing patrol was enough. The result was constant flapping between developing
and defending, which kept resetting the timer that eventually forces an attack,
so both sides sat at home until the clock ran out.
af9295558816:
Swapping bases: a conditional gamble for the top difficulty, distinct from
turning an attack around mid-retreat. It fires when the enemy's main force is
pressing our base and we cannot win the fight there, but their centre is known to
be weakly defended and ours can hold without us. It acts on memory, since by then
we usually cannot see their base at all.
6ba90b8e76bf:
Commitment to a pounce. Once an all-in attack on the enemy centre is under way,
the commitment window holds even if the situation at home turns defensive --
otherwise sending the main force out immediately spikes the home threat, the
main force is recalled, and it never once reaches the enemy centre. That recall
loop was why bases were never actually destroyed.
b0d84cb84328:
The machine-learned brain, at the top difficulties only: the predicted mode
drives the state machine and the investment split drives resource weighting, with
a fallback to the rule-based brain. Commitment windows are respected -- a
committed gamble is not interrupted -- and the army-wiped safety clause still
applies.
3ec1c6f8d68a:
Early warning at the top difficulty: raise the alarm only when an attack is
confirmed, not merely suspected. With the threat not yet at the door but a
sizeable force confirmed advancing, the one defensive group that is normally away
from home -- the patrol -- is recalled to the rally point to meet it.
24dca52e05a3:
Patrols cover the home zone, and only that. Doubling them up as feint squads
never worked: the feint order was overwritten by this branch's patrol order on
the very next tick, so they never once reached the enemy economy.
506af37a744b:
Escorts have to march with the siege, not merely at the same time. The old
threshold counted total strength, and two catapults alone exceeded it -- so an
unescorted siege group set off by itself and arrived as targets. When a column
contains siege, the threshold counts escorting combat strength instead.
8a638fefa35b:
Leaving together is not the same as travelling together: a catapult moves at 45
against infantry's 70, so on a large map the column stretches into a line, the
escort arrives and merges with the main force, and the siege finishes the journey
alone.
37bf811c0613:
Completing the pounce. All-in means everything at the enemy centre, but the old
code only created that mission when no mission existed -- so an economic mission
from the pressure phase carried straight into the all-in, and the main force kept
attacking farms while the enemy centre sat at full health all game.
8ca6022105c8:
Once the main force is deep in enemy territory, re-targeting no longer routes it
back through its own rally point, so it can shift from one point to the next
without running home in between.
2ab4c825d7a9:
Retreat test: if the enemy-to-friendly strength ratio within 380px of the centroid
exceeds the threshold, this fight is lost -- pull back home, with a cooldown to
stop it flapping. A threshold of zero means the easier difficulties never retreat.
f76bd2f99ae2:
Turning a retreat into an attack. Losing to the enemy's main force means their
main force is out here -- and their base is not. It requires all three: we are
closer to their base than they are, their base really is far from them, and ours
can hold. Then instead of going home we go for their centre.
fe98b5ec64e5:
Nibbling cannot apply only on the final leg of a march. An all-in mission's legs
are [staging point, enemy centre], and the main force almost never leaves the
first one before the window closes -- so the intermediate target was chosen
correctly and nobody ever went to attack it. The real meaning is "once the outer
edge of the enemy defensive ring is reached".
63f06c298fe8:
Regrouping when badly hurt. A half-strength army that keeps pushing without
healing is wiped out, and then there is nothing left on the field and the next
wave has to start from nothing. The previous rule only stopped in place during a
lull, with no disengage-heal-return loop, so the main force stayed glued to the
front at half health until the next contact killed it.
cadc2c6f3ca4:
Gather latch for siege. Staged marching only checked cohesion at the moment the
centroid reached a staging point, so between staging points -- often dozens of
tiles -- the fast units still outran the catapults, and the escort was fiction by
the time the enemy line was reached. Now cohesion is checked while marching.
0c0ad1dbcc3d:
Siege actually arriving. Before this, catapults fired zero shots at buildings all
game, on both sides: the siege group was assigned the mission's final target --
an enemy centre eighty tiles away -- and focus fire only applies inside the
engagement radius, so they neither focused on anything nor got there. They now
share the same intermediate target as the main force.
fe5f924508c7:
Muster point: on our own frontier, offset towards the target, and outside the
firing rings of any known enemy tower, centre or castle. Aiming it along the line
to the target matters -- using the main road instead meant mustering with your
back to the objective whenever the objective was not on that road.
988bca66ffdf:
Known enemy towns: anchored on enemy centres, cities and outposts that have
actually been seen, with anchors within ten tiles merged into one town. Each town
is scored for weakness by its known defences plus observed troops, in the same
spirit as the local defence score but at town scale. The scan order is by tile
coordinate, which keeps it deterministic.
d25f51a111b8:
Forward staging post: a set distance back from the target, offset towards where
the wave is coming from, and pushed further back if it would sit inside known
enemy fire.
cd7ea348ae80:
Choosing the wave's target: walled in, breach the wall first; otherwise pick
among known towns by the difficulty's rule -- the opponent's main base, the
nearest by real path length rather than straight line, or the weakest. With no
known town, the nearest known enemy economy; with nothing at all, nothing, and
the scouts go looking. Cached for a few seconds, because the path-length variant
is not cheap.
fe16b89d2948:
Three-squad production: fill the home guard's fixed quota first (casualties take
priority), then patrols, then accumulate the reserve for the next wave, capped at
the all-in size so a wave is big enough to matter. An attack force already in the
field is not topped up -- the reserve merges into it as a new wave instead.
dec10ee67671:
The fourth squad, at the top difficulties only, and only once the main force is
above its safety line: splitting off a raiding party that makes the main wave too
weak is worse than not raiding. It prefers cavalry, which is the formation that
goes straight for villagers.
c4047c089f5d:
Early production is split roughly evenly between military and development.
While cumulative military spending stays under a multiple of cumulative
development spending, it bypasses the reservation ledger (the cash still has to
exist) -- otherwise economic reservations block military production for the whole
early game, which probing convicted as the cause of a reserve force that stayed
at zero for the first seven minutes.
The allowance is self-balancing: it accrues as the economy really spends, so
military spending trails development and can never starve it.
95b71fa623a4:
Three-squad orchestration: intelligence and threat scanning first, then the wave
lifecycle (develop, muster, launch), the mandatory gather, no retreat for the
attack force with a timed fallback home, and finally march or attack mode.
The tactical layer is unchanged underneath: kiting, sheltering when hurt,
nibbling at the perimeter, siege positioning, and pulling out cut-off units.
48bf328f1afb: Home threat scan, with memory of raided resources.
8212c66fd775: Launch threshold, tied to the all-in size so it is always reachable.
a78b994ab591:
Home in real danger: the guard and reserve together cannot hold. The threshold is
set at a real attack, so a small raid does not flip the whole brain onto the
defensive.
3f2a1751a2ef: Reserve gathers into a wave, then launches.
4e994fb92b9a:
Home guard: garrison plus patrol, kept back as a buffer alongside the towers and
the town centre's fire.
c13c470ebe26:
In contact: any unit in the wave has been fired on in the last few seconds.
Attacking villagers does not count, since villagers do not shoot back.
1a61295f7eb3:
Meeting an enemy on the march means fighting it -- running into an army on the
road is not a reason to go home. At the top difficulties a scattered wave pulls
back one step to bunch up first, with a timeout so it cannot back up forever;
below that it simply engages.
63278741c897:
Gathering before arrival. A wave counts as gathered when at least 70% of it is
within eight tiles of its centroid; otherwise it falls back to the staging post
and waits, with a timeout. Changing target mid-march goes through the same
sequence: pull back, gather, then attack.
47a22561cb01:
Marching under guard orders rather than attack-move, so the column does not
chase every distraction on the way; whether to kite is decided separately.
8d1be89fcebd:
Scouts explore by default even at the top difficulty; only when the picture of
the enemy base has gone stale is one scout manually sent to an observation point
-- outside the known firing rings, but with line of sight to the enemy centre --
for a look. Once it arrives, or the intelligence is fresh again, it goes back to
exploring on its own.
926baf3871ac:
Known enemy defensive strength near a point: sighted fortifications, weighted,
plus sighted troops. It reads only that side's own intelligence, so unscouted
ground scores zero -- picking the weak point is what scouting buys, not a peek at
the map.
